summary:
Given her age, active RA requiring medication, normal ferritin despite low hemoglobin & MCV indicating possible ongoing inflammatory state rather than severe iron depletion alone; thus suggesting a likely case of *Anemia Of Chronic Disease*. Key differentiating factors include elevated ferritin levels which are typically seen more prominently in this condition compared to simple iron-deficiency states where it tends to be reduced due to increased hepcidin production suppressing intestinal absorption as well as reticuloendothelial cell recycling leading towards overall decreased body stores over time if not replenished adequately through diet/parenteral means especially when coupled alongside autoimmune conditions such as Rheumatoid Arthritis itself being associated often enough clinically too! 

The patient’s profile strongly supports diagnosing them with 'Anemia Due To Underlying Inflammatory Condition' instead of just plain ‘Iron Deficient Anaemia’. This conclusion hinges upon recognizing how certain markers behave differently across various types of anaemias particularly those linked closely via immune-inflammatory pathways versus nutritional deficiencies per se!
To refine further:

A 25-year-old woman treated for rheumatoid arthritis presents with microcytic anemia characterized by low hemoglobin, small mean corpuscular volume consistent with either iron deficiency or anemia of chronic disease. Her raised ferritin level distinguishes anemia of chronic disease, making it the most plausible cause given her systemic inflammatory condition. Thus, she probably suffers from "Anemia of Chronic Disease."
